narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses whether applicants are likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, estimated a 71.1% probability that you would fail it. This high risk of failure triggered the rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting students with strong odds of bar success to maintain its standards. The model's decision hinged most on your academic performance in school, particularly your low ranking in Year 3 grades—placing you in the bottom decile compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack position—which strongly pushed the prediction toward failure by signaling potential struggles with demanding legal coursework. Your Year 1 grades, also in the bottom decile well below average, reinforced this concern as the second-biggest factor, suggesting early academic challenges that often persist. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.10, slightly below the average of 3.21, actually worked in your favor, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing and showing some resilience in college-level work. As a Black male from a middle-income family planning full-time studies—much like the vast majority of applicants—your LSAT score of 28, far below the typical 36.7, played a supporting role but wasn't the top driver here. Compared to other students, your consistently low school deciles stand out as unusually weak predictors of bar success, unlike peers who climb into higher rankings. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag based on patterns from thousands of past students. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your high school performance through additional coursework or tutoring if possible, and highlighting any upward trends or extenuating circumstances in a reapplication. Building a stronger academic narrative could shift your predicted odds significantly for future cycles. You're clearly motivated to pursue law, and with targeted improvements, schools will see your true capabilities. Keep pushing—many have turned similar profiles around.
